> Any thoughts on why putting +Inf on the last bucket value? My intent with interpolating a higher value so it moves the needle as much as previous midpoints. Again... if many values are ending in the +Inf bucket that's considered a bad config.

I assume this question is for me, based on the description in my initial comment. It's not `+Inf` on the last bucket, but the second last bucket's upper bound. I mainly chose that following what Prometheus's `histogram_quantile` function does:

> If a quantile is located in the highest bucket, the upper bound of the second highest bucket is returned.

My thinking is that since we don't have any insight into that last bucket, so reporting anything greater than the second last bucket's upper limit risks overstating the value. Reporting the second last bucket's upper limit is a bit more conservative, ensuring that we report a valid lower bound for percentile queries that fall beyond the configured buckets.

This is identical to canonicalization for ``kl_div`` (reproduced below)
```
def kl_div_canon(expr, args):
    shape = expr.shape
    x = promote(args[0], shape)
    y = promote(args[1], shape)
    t = Variable(shape)
    constraints = [ExpCone(t, x, y)]
    obj = y - x - t
    return obj, constraints
```

> I do not want to block the operation of the gui and control of other instruments while this pend occurs.

Absolutely! I think this _the_ classical case of the GUI and business logic being separated and happening in separate threads. E.g. when using pyqt this is pretty standard. Don't know your specifics, but i found [this book](https://www.packtpub.com/product/mastering-gui-programming-with-python/9781789612905) to be quite approachable.

The way I see it, the concurrency stuff should not be part of the driver, but sit in the application layer, i.e. between your GUI code and the instrument drivers. 

In your case I could see you configuring a move with GUI element, then having a button "Start slew!". When pressed, it kicks of the concurrent move(s). Futures have a [way of notifying](https://docs.python.org/3.9/library/concurrent.futures.html#concurrent.futures.Future.add_done_callback) via callback when they're done, you can probably use that to then re-activate the button or make an indicator green or unlock the "Expose" button or whatnot - most GUI frameworks revolve around callbacks (signals/slots in Qt lingo).

The trade-off is that `mTimer` will expire more often. In contrast, with the current code, it only expires when a TCP timer expires. For context, TCP will set its retransmit timer every RTT, so now we will have `mTimer` expiring once every RTT, even in the common case where the ACK arrives and TCP resets the timer.  The trade-off is what you consider to be expensive.  In previous platforms I've worked with, waking up the CPU from sleep was expensive, so I wrote the timer code to avoid expiring the timer unless we absolutely had to. For OpenThread it's unclear, so I went with your simpler alternative for now. It looks like the default alarm driver wakes up the CPU periodically anyway so it won't make a difference. But if the driver is ever changed to manage the timer in a tickless way, it might be worth revisiting this.

Note that we shouldn't define a default option by `OPENTHREAD_FTD`, `OPENTHREAD_MTD` or `OPENTHREAD_RADIO`. The reason is that they (FTD, MTD, RADIO) are only visible to OpenThread core. `otPlatRadioConfigureEnhAckProbing` is defined in platform and wrapped by `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_LINK_METRICS_SUBJECT_ENABLE`. When building it, `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_LINK_METRICS_SUBJECT_ENABLE` evaluates to `0` because there is no definition of `OPENTHREAD_FTD`.

We actually met this issue before. And we elimiated the warnings by not defining default options y thins like `OPENTHREAD_FTD`. See #5923 and #5428

In this case, we can remove `OPENTHREAD_FTD` here and replace the occurences of guard `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_LINK_METRICS_SUBJECT_ENABLE` to `OPENTHREAD_FTD && OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_MLE_LINK_METRICS_SUBJECT_ENABLE`. We must replace that in `ot-nrf528xx` first. I apologize I didn't do this before.

The TCPlp timer callbacks are TCP functions. Most user-specified callbacks are called in response to receiving a packet, which is why I hadn't thought about this earlier... but thinking about what you said, there is one callback--- `connection_lost`---that might be called in response to a timer event, if it causes the whole TCP connection to time out.

So I agree that we need to write this more defensively, since the callback might enter user code, which may update timers on the same TCP endpoint and/or the other endpoints, or even mutate the linked list. This is what I think we can assume:
* Any newly set timers won't have expired yet, since TCPlp will only set timers to expire in the future (including some minimum delay)
* Any newly created TCP endpoints won't have any expired timers (corollary of above), but they might have active timers (if the user code ended up calling `otTcpEndpointConnect` or something).

I think that either of the two approaches you suggested (the first approach is to `FireAtIfEarlier`, the second approach is to iterate over the `Endpoint`s twice) would work. For now, I've just used `FireAtIfEarlier` since that's more efficient.

@rly I have to admit, this is a bit of a tricky problem. The case ``if isinstance(vals, np.ndarray)`` occurs when the data is being read from disk. In this case ``self.data`` will be a ``hdmf.backends.hdf5.h5_utils.ContainerH5TableDataset`` and the data is read from file as a numpy structured array. In contrast, when we create the file, the compound data is treated as a list of tuples. This means that the structure of the data is quite different in those  two cases.  So when calling ``np.asarray(vals)`` we get:
* A numpy array with ``dtype=object`` and ``shape=(1,3)`` when ``self.data`` is a list of tuples
* A numpy array with ``dtype=[('idx_start', '<i4'), ('count', '<i4'), ('timeseries', 'O')] `` and ``shape=(1,)`` when ``self.data`` is read from file. 

This leads to two key issues:

* Dealing with the difference in shape (i.e., 1D vs 2D array) downstream is annoying
* Dealing with the difference in dtype downstream is annoying
* Conversion to pandas DataFrame fails with the structured dtype with the error shown below.

In part because of the latter issue I had opted for converting the data on read to a list of tuples to keep things consistent. I agree that this should be documented more clearly in the code to avoid confusion later on.

How does VectorData usually deal with compound types? Are they treated as 2D arrays or as 1D structured arrays? Ideally we should try to keep the behavior here consistent with how other Container classes treat compound datatypes. If we should use a structured array instead (which may be more intuitive) it would require: 1) converting ``vals`` to a structured array if it is a list of tuples (essentially the opposite of the current logic) and 2) require dealing with the conversion to pandas issues downstream. I'm not sure yet what it will take to deal with the conversion issue. Pandas appears to attempt a reduction on the masked array which fails to deal with the complex dtype. 

What are your thoughts? What should the right behavior be?

> I'm not suggesting to change the default of weights, it's fine to leave it as None.

I understood that. Sorry I should have been more explicit on how I made the link between `weights='pretrained'` and the default values of `weights` in the signature.

The reason I linked the two is because IMO having a non-None value as default has exactly the same effect as supporting something like `weights='pretrained'`. They anchor us to a specific old and potentially superseded set of weights. That's why I'm in favour of supporting still a `weights=<something>` but that something shouldn't be constant across all models but rather explicitly define which set of weights to load. This is currently already supported because the weights can be a string with the name of the Enum.

In other words, I'm hoping to avoid introducing anything on the API that will anchor the user to legacy weights. I'm happy to support a `weights='latest'` thought, for those who are happy to always access the best available weights for a specific model architecture.

My implementation also does not re-download the archives, if they are already present. Within the `download_and_extract_tar` the following lines achieve this:
```python
if not check_integrity(os.path.join(download_root, filename), md5):
    download_url(url, download_root, filename=filename, md5=md5)
```
Thus, it is only re-downloaded if
1. the archive is not present in the given root directory, or
2. its MD5 hash does not check out.

With the development kit however (see discussion above) we could do some integrity checks:
1. Assert that `train` and `val` folder contain exactly `1000` subfolders with the correct wordnet IDs, and

Including errcode:

* Delete the default copy constructor and `operator=`. Doing this may (you have to try) require you to define the other constructors and assignments.

Ok let's put things that way.

You have 3 possibles ways to write content in a file in the tests, that leads to three difference encoding:
1) using `open("/path/to/file")` in Python: UTF-8
2) builtin CMD write (`output-command >> /path/to/file`): CP1252 (or other depending of the region)
3) builtin Powershell write (`& output-command >> /path/to/file`): UTF-16-LE

In parallel Pyton will assume `UTF-8` in `open` calls that end up reading a file, if not using specific flags or encoding.

On master method 1) is use in the tests when we invoke hooks by files, like it is done in `test_renew_with_hook_scripts`. And method 2) is used each time we have something like `misc.echo('wtf_pre', context.hook_probe)` in parameters for hook flags during a certbot call, since the `echo` method is defined as below:
https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/4cce3458f3e6a38fbb3f39613aca1c9e1ebd86ca/certbot-ci/certbot_integration_tests/utils/misc.py#L290-L302

What changes my PR is since hooks commands are not executed with Powershell, the method 3) is effectively used when `echo` function is involved, and the probe files then have `UTF-16-LE` encoding instead of `CP1252`.

At the end, all probe files are asserted with `assert_hook_execution` method, that is invoking `open` and so read all files in `UTF-8`.

Despite having `UTF-8` or `CP1252` encoding probe files on master, things were working well, because `CP1252` is single-byte encoding, and UTF-8 also for ANSI-like characters, which is only what we use here. And `CP1252`, similarly to `UTF-8` had the elegancy to use the same byte value than the ANSI encoding, so at the end everything is correctly read with just assuming everything is `UTF-8`.

However `UTF-16-LE` is a double-byte encoding. Here forget about ANSI encoding retrocompatibility. Then our tests are failing when `assert_hook_execution` tries to read the files written with method 3) assuming `UTF-8` encoding.

There were two possibilities to fix that issue:
- change the logic as I did in this PR, to always have `UTF-16-LE` encoding and assume that when we are on Windows for the tests
- change `echo` function to write probes from Python using `open`, and continue to assume everything is `UTF-8`

I prefered the first solution because I did not want to rely to much on Python to cover the plateform differences, since these integration tests are precisely here to detect errors on specific plateforms. In particular I wanted to be more "real world" with Certbot hooks, as on Windows, everything is embedded and you cannot assume that a Certbot user has in fact a Python distribution installed on his system. Using the builtin shell file writes felt a decent logic to test. Finally the first solution allows to continue to have a minimal logic written in something else than Python.

Now I think there is still something to do. At least we need to have the line `[Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = ([Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol -bor [Net.SecurityProtocolType]::Tls12)`, because it will enable the use of TLS 1.2 at least on all systems, or a better TLS version if it is available.

Now if GitHub decides on day to accept only TLS 1.3, this code will not break either if:
* the systems have all TLS 1.3 available and use it by default
* or we update the current script to enable TLS 1.3 or better, with `[Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = ([Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol -bor [Net.SecurityProtocolType]::Tls13)`

However we cannot do anything for systems that do not have TLS 1.3 available.

There's three cases:
1) Both the Jacobian and Hessian are provided: Do nothing special
2) Only the Jacobian is provided: approximate the Hessian using a complex step finite difference ('cs')
3) Neither is provided: approximate the Jacobian using a complex step finite difference ('cs'). In that case scipy doesn't let us approximate the Hessian using finite difference, and instead forces you to use a numerical optimization. There are 2 minimizers that can do this, `scipy.optimize.BFGS` and `SR1`. SR1 blew up in my face so I abandoned that one. `so.BFGS` requires an option to be passed for `exception_strategy`.

This is also the reason why execute must accept a `jacobian` and `hessian` kwarg for the user to override this behaviour, e.g. when their function can not be evaluated in the complex plane.

Note: all this is only valid for the `trust-constr` minimizer. So maybe I'll move it there.

Originally went down this route as well. However what I discovered is that in Python 2 some things only support the old-style buffer protocol and some support the new buffer protocol or both. Admittedly Python 3 is much more straightforward as everything supports the new  buffer protocol where possible.

Given this realization it seemed much better to return something that supported both protocols and was generally more functional (to support reshaping, casting, type checking, etc.). Fortunately NumPy's `ndarray` does all of these things. So it just made more sense to always convert to that. Plus contributors are probably more comfortable with NumPy `ndarray`s than `memoryview`s.

2. Nobody needs this documentation. Nobody should be catching assertion errors.
3. If I had to document every assertion error I raise, I might be discouraged to make assertions. Python code I've seen most often has fewer assertions that I'd like it to.

I was learning from https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/modules.html#optional-module-loading-and-other-advanced-loading-scenarios and some experimentation.

When an import is "compile time only" like importing Interface types, it gets elided during translation to JavaScript.

So the pattern is:

```
import * as foo from "foo";  // compile-time only import IFF no foo.func() calls are made; erased from JS code

....
    if (cond) {
         var actualFoo: typeof foo = require("foo");  // require() gets compiled to actual JS code
         // since the typeof declaration above, actualFoo.callFunc() gets type-checked properly, it's not <any>
    }


```

- If the timer is not started to execute yet, it won't be run.
- If the timer is executing right now, it will try to finish its execution(in the reactor thread, but we are not waiting for it right there). In the timer, before trying to `get_or_connect` to a member, we do have a `lifecycle.running` check. (which will be `False` after the shutdown call). If we can pass this check in the reactor thread, we can infact create a connection. We do have a cleanup code in the `reactor.shutdown` but it is cleaning up connection before executing the timers. (it may create connection after entering the loop in which we iterate over connections) Therefore, I moved waiting for timers to finish before cleaning up the connections. After reading the spec again, I have some interesting findings and questions now. Correct me if I'm wrong.

First I must say, currently my description of **'CSL channel has been specified or not'** is inaccurate. The spec actually means:
1. When *macCSLChannelMask* has value `0`, CSL tx uses `phyCurrentChannel`.
2. When *macCSLChannelMask* isn't `0`, CSL tx uses `phyCurrentChannel`.

**I would call these two conditions case 1 and case 2 below.**

*macCSLChannelMask* is a PIB attribute. So if we set CSL Channel first(move to case 2) and then want to get back to case 1, we just need to change *macCSLChannelMask* back to 0. I believe that `The value of phyCurrentChannel when macCSLChannelMask has value 0.` indicates *macCSLChannelMask* could equal to 0.

The spec also mentions that(**3.2.6.3.2**):

> The macCslChannelMask is relayed by a CSL Receiver SSED Child to a CSL Transmitter Parent Router via the CSL Channel TLV sent in the Child Update Request as described in Section 4.6

However, what you mentioned(CSL Channel TLV in 4.4.31) is right. If we set `CslChannelPage` to `0`, then we cannot set `CslChannel` to `0` in the TLV.

2. If by no means we can relay `0` in CSL Channel TLV, then I think we cannot use a 'Thread' way to move from case 2 to case 1. We have to set *macCSLChannelMask* on both sides if we want to do so. Actually I think it's fine even if we don't support to move from case 2 to case 1. Just ask for your opinion.

For now, I would prefer implement it all conforming to the spec. That is, we cannot move from case 2 to case 1. Thoughts?

> E.g., we could make a PythonArtifactBase union and call await Get(SetupKwargs, PythonArtifactBase, target.provides)

For this particular implementation, this breaks the Target API because `target.provides` is a `PythonProvidesField`. We encourage subclassing of `Field`s, and that needs to continue working, i.e. that you can subclass a `Field` without breaking any functionality.

But, I think that your suggestion is actually about subclassing `PythonArtifact`, right? Sure, we could do that. That was one of the original designs I considered. But I think it has several downsides compared to this approach:

1. Users must subclass `PythonArtifact`, including registering it in the legacy `BuildFileAliases` type (which we want to move away from). They also must remember to use that subclass in their BUILD files, or they'll accidentally not get their plugin behavior.
2. There's less flexibility for plugin authors to decide when their implementation should be used. For example, a plugin author may _always_ want their implementation to be used, like we do in this repo—so they can return `True` to `is_valid()`. Or, a plugin author might want to do something like inspect `target.address` so that all addresses in folder `project1/` use plugin1, and all addresses in `project2/` use `plugin2`. Or, they might want it to be based on the identity of the `PythonArtificat`, which they can still do via `is_valid()`. We let the plugin author decide what makes sense for them, rather than requiring that they create a subclass and BUILD file authors remember to use the correct subclass.

> How about you just take the plugin string from the action payload instead?

This makes sense for `changePlugin` actions, but not really for `pluginsListingLoaded`.  The latter's payload is just a list of plugins, and only the CoreReducer is responsible for knowing how to update the `activePlugin`.  Having the analytics code know how pluginsListingLoaded selects a default plugin doesn't make sense.

The core reason for the before/after check is that anyone dispatching `changePlugin` doesn't know whether it will actually change the active plugin, so our metaReducer must check itself.  

That said, maybe it's not so bad, having a metaReducer assume that inner reducers may affect the result of a selector in some way?  (unless this is an anti-pattern or some principle is violated)

Modern versions of `buildozer` allow for roughly:
```
$ buildozer 'print sources' src/scala/com/example:example
rglobs("*.scala")
$ buildozer 'remove sources' src/scala/com/example:example
fixed src/scala/com/example/BUILD
$ buildozer 'add sources "*.scala"' src/scala/com/example:example
fixed src/scala/com/example/BUILD
$ diff
-    sources = rglobs("*.scala"),
+    sources = ["*.scala"],
```

see https://github.com/bazelbuild/buildtools/blob/master/buildozer/README.md. A caveat is that it will reformat files files for you, and doesn't support refactoring syntax that is illegal in bazel (imports, for example).

This is removing the gaps from the input data to make it contiguous by repacking the data.

If the number of bits in the input is a multiple of `KEY_BITS`, then `bloom.bitfield` will be 1/8 the size of `contents` in memory. This would be awful if all of the bits in `contents` were used, but because of the bug, each item in `contents` is only using one byte, and the others are all 0 / never written to.

Pulling from the tests, it works a bit like this... (this is similar to the explanation above)

```
AA000000 BB000000 CC000000 DD000000
... converted to ...
AABBCCDD
```

In this case, `block` is which container index to go to, and `idx` is the offset within the container.

```python
>>>	channel = Channel(...)
...
>>>	async with channel as ch:

...		  r1 = await client.bar(...)

Hm, do you think we should call it simply `Specs`? I had two reasons for using `ParsedSpecs`:
1) Distinguish between raw spec strings vs. parsed specs
   * But, raw specs are much more limited in their usage. Probably better for `Specs` to default to meaning the parsed `AddressSpecs` and `FilesystemSpecs` and exclusively using `raw specs` or `spec strings` to describe the unparsed arguments provided by users.
2) Reserve the word `Spec` because we aren't yet sure if that should exist and what it should look like.
    * However, after thinking about it, we probably would end up having a `Spec` superclass for one single FilesystemSpec vs. AddressSpec, but implement `Specs` as a dataclass just like `ParsedSpecs` is now, rather than as a `Collection[Spec]`.

So, I think you're right to call it Specs.

cc @stuhood on this to confirm.

`await Get(Targets, Addresses)` expands out all "file targets" every time. Meaning, it generates a more granular target which only has one source file, rather than the original "BUILD target" you see in the BUILD file. Run `./pants peek --output=json src/python/pants/util:util` - surprisingly, you'll get >1 target.

Almost certainly, we instead want to use `await Get(UnexpandedTargets, Addresses, addresses)`. This will only give back the precise target specified by the user, i.e. `src/python/pants/util:util` gives you that BUILD target, and then `src/python/pants/util/strutil.py` gives you the more granular file target `strutil.py`.

@stuhood can you think of any scenario here where we wouldn't want to use `UnexapandedTargets`?

If you’ve come this far in your search for automation, we’re guessing you’re familiar with packages as well as their role in customizing a Salesforce org. However, because most everything that is built in CumulusCI is deployed via packages, it’s essential to clarify once more:

A **package** is a container for something as small as an individual component or as large as a set of related apps. After creating a package, you can distribute it to other Salesforce users and organizations, including those outside your company.

**Unmanaged packages** are typically used to distribute open-source (non-proprietary) features or application templates to provide developers with the basic building blocks for an application. These freshly installed components can be edited as seen fit by the owners of this implementation. The developer who created and uploaded the unmanaged package has no control over the installed components, and can't change or upgrade them.
 
**Managed packages** are typically used by Salesforce partners to distribute and sell applications to customers. They are proprietary code (apps?) that can only be upgraded and deployed by the developer that built them. To ensure seamless upgrades, certain destructive changes, like removing objects or fields, cannot be performed.

Packages are also built, stored and deployed via a **project**.

```py
def test_binding_strategy():

    # The confusion here is in the two arguments “override” and “replace” and
    # how they serve two different purposes.

    # 1. Replacing a binding:
    # Say they release FOAF 2.0 and you want to change `foaf` to point to `http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/`.

    # Up to now you had `foaf=>http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/` and `foaf2=>http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/`
    g = Graph(bind_namespaces="none")
    assert len(list(g.namespaces())) == 0

    foaf1_uri = URIRef('http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/')
    foaf2_uri = URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/")

    FOAF1 = Namespace(foaf1_uri)
    FOAF2 = Namespace(foaf2_uri)

    g.bind("foaf", FOAF1)
    g.bind("foaf2", FOAF2)
    assert len(list(g.namespaces())) == 2
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [('foaf', foaf1_uri), ("foaf2", foaf2_uri)]

    # Now you want to "upgrade" to foaf=>FOAF 2.0.
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF2)

    # But that doesn't happen, instead a different prefix is bound:
    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [("foaf", foaf1_uri), ("foaf1", foaf2_uri)]

    # The prefix "foaf" was already bound to the FOAF1 namespace, so a new
    # prefix for FOAF2 was created.

    # You can achieve the intended result by setting `replace` to `True`:
    g.bind("foaf", FOAF2, replace=True)

    # Because the FOAF2 namespace was rebound to a different prefix, the old 
    # "foaf2" prefix has gone and because the "foaf" prefix was rebound to a
    # different namespace, the old FOAF1 namespace has gone, leaving just:

    assert list(g.namespaces()) == [("foaf", URIRef("http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/"))]

@tseaver

> I don't see how any URL generation using localhost and whatever port number we are getting from None can possibly yield valid URLs.

Right now we would generate it using `localhost` as the `SERVER_NAME` and `/tmp/waitress.test-43692.sock` as `SERVER_PORT` which would yield the following URL's:

`http://localhost:/tmp/waitress.test-43692.sock/other/path/info`.

Which is a "valid" URL.

I'd much rather we create URL's using "localhost" than "unix". "localhost" is guaranteed to resolve to 127.0.0.1, `unix` may resolve to whatever happens to be out there on the internet.

If the URL is returned to a user with `unix` set in the URL, and they try to connect to it, they will do a DNS request for `unix` followed by `unix.<whatever search path they have setup for their system's DNS>`.

From my logs at home while trying to run a curl `http://unix/`.

```
Sep  6 19:43:23 Breached unbound: [30770:0] info: 10.10.10.130 unix.home.arpa. A IN NXDOMAIN 0.000000 1 82
Sep  6 19:43:23 Breached unbound: [30770:0] info: 10.10.10.130 unix. A IN NXDOMAIN 0.000000 1 97
```

For example:

```
Riley:~ xistence$ dig unix

; <<>> DiG 9.10.6 <<>> unix
;; global options: +cmd
;; Got answer:
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NXDOMAIN, id: 43892
;; flags: qr rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 0, AUTHORITY: 1, ADDITIONAL: 1

;; OPT PSEUDOSECTION:
; EDNS: version: 0, flags:; udp: 4096
;; QUESTION SECTION:
;unix.				IN	A

;; AUTHORITY SECTION:
.			3600	IN	SOA	a.root-servers.net. nstld.verisign-grs.com. 2018090601 1800 900 604800 86400

;; Query time: 96 msec
;; SERVER: 10.10.10.11#53(10.10.10.11)
;; WHEN: Thu Sep 06 13:43:15 MDT 2018
;; MSG SIZE  rcvd: 108
```

Went all the way to the root DNS servers just to get a negative response.

Whereas gets resolved locally:

```
Riley:~ xistence$ dig localhost

; <<>> DiG 9.10.6 <<>> localhost
;; global options: +cmd
;; Got answer:
;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 29739
;; flags: qr aa rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1

;; OPT PSEUDOSECTION:
; EDNS: version: 0, flags:; udp: 4096
;; QUESTION SECTION:
;localhost.			IN	A

;; ANSWER SECTION:
localhost.		10800	IN	A	127.0.0.1

;; Query time: 66 msec
;; SERVER: 10.10.10.11#53(10.10.10.11)
;; WHEN: Thu Sep 06 13:43:29 MDT 2018
;; MSG SIZE  rcvd: 54
```

This just means potential clients aren't connecting to the internet/asking for information that is invalid.

I am not suggesting it's the best behaviour, but I do think it is better than arbitrarily using `unix` for `SERVER_NAME`. As I mentioned, I've created/opened a new ticket to fix the behaviour as best as possible by using information provided by the proxy server that sits in front of the unix socket, so that the information is correct.

@dbfreem thanks. I did some research and have the following points to highlight:

1. A code fragment which requires exclusive access to a data structure must not release a lock until the work is done. I mean acquiring the lock in multiple touches is not correct since there may be concurrent access between them. And any access should be protected by a lock, including the `in` lookup. IMO these lines must be protected by a single `acquire` https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/6c845b6eddcff2f2180829b0084fc63e0aae604b/web3/_utils/request.py#L89-L92

2. That leads us to the second point. Now I suspect what might be the reason of deadlocks you mentioned. We can't just wrap the lines above with the lock since it'll stumble upon the second `acquire` in `cache_session`. That's because `Lock` is not reentrant. You might re-arrange the code or use `threading.RLock` to overcome this.

3. Also I remembered about a note here https://docs.python.org/3/library/threading.html#threading.Lock.acquire : "Changed in version 3.2: Lock acquisition can now be interrupted by signals on POSIX if the underlying threading implementation supports it.". IMO that means it's not safe to ignore a return value of `acquire`, meaning that it might not be safe to use `Lock` or `RLock` as a context manager as-is. I'd rewrite it with a helper:
```python
@contextmanager
def locked(lock):
    """
    Contextmanager which locks and releases a lock in the safest way
    :param lock: Lock or RLock
    """
    while not lock.acquire():   # may be interrupted by a signal, so a loop is needed
        pass

    try:
        yield
    finally:
        lock.release()
```

> One of the python mantra is that there should be multiple ways to do it.

From the [zen of python](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/) also known as PEP20:

> There should be one-- and preferably only one --obvious way to do it.

I mean, users can use `functools.partial` if they want, nothing in the API proposed in this document prevents them from doing so, but I wouldn't put it in the docs, nor I would encourage it by adding convenience functions. It hurts readability. 

Someone reading your code might have problems reproducing your results because he/she didn't expect the signature of a function to change.

A good API shouldn't push towards using magic. If you want to use magic stuff like `functools.partial`, you can subclass `Config` or `Experiment` and add whatever method you want. This is where the flexibility of the current proposal is handy.

In case a legacy Leader does not CoAP-ACK the request (because it does not recognize the kBorderRouterRequest status), there are also CoAP retransmissions sent.  A legacy Leader could also ACK the request but not send the response. Or it could ACK the request and send a response status of 'No Address Available'.  All of these cases may have a different timeout/retransmission behavior. Good to keep in mind.

In any case if the ADDR_SOL.req message has been ACK'ed by the Leader the BR but no Success response then the BR can at least assume the Leader's not willing to grant the request and could stop sending these -- until the Leader/Partition changes.

If the ADDR_SOL.req message is not ACK'ed by the Leader then the BR may also stop sending (like above) just for consistency and treat it as a denial of the request.   (Even though officially in CoAP this situation means that the CoAP request was never received by the Leader.)

Another solution could be to only retry such request again when the number of Routers in the Partition has changed. I.e. use that as a simple trigger to avoid sending requests in vain whenever some minor thing in the network data is changing.   New Leader/Partition is in any case a trigger to send the request again.

I personally think we could take this:

```python
ChecksumAddress = NewType('ChecksumAddress', HexAddress)
AnyAddress = TypeVar('AnyAddress', Address, HexAddress, ChecksumAddress)
```

and turn it into this:

```python
ChecksumAddress = NewType('ChecksumAddress', HexAddress, str)
AnyAddress = TypeVar('AnyAddress', Address, HexAddress, ChecksumAddress, str)
```

After all, these `NewType`'s are just to define the intend of the underlying data and trace that it is used correctly across function boundaries.

So you might look at this and say: "Wait, but then we can define `ChecksumAddress("Foo")` which is clearly wrong. But I'd argue that you can do the same today just in a slightly more involved form of `CheckSumAddress(HexAddress(HexStr("Foo")))`. So, there is always a "trust gate" at the point where the `NewType` is created. The `NewType` can not actually check if this is a 20-byte string. It can only trace if the correct `NewType` flows from method to method. At the point where the `NewType` is being created we have to put some trust into the developer who's wrapping whatever type as a `NewType`.

file reader as an argument and own it, and then just expose a
`read_record(&mut self)` method that calls the underlying reader.

For instance, `BufReader` in the standard library does this:
<https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/io/struct.BufReader.html#method.new>

If you let a `TfRecordReader` own and wrap an underlying reader, then
you have an additional guarantee: nothing else can access the underlying
reader anymore. For instance, in Java, you could write this:

```java
FileReader fr = new FileReader("/etc/hosts");
BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(br);
br.readLine();
fr.read();  // poke into the underlying reader!
br.readLine();  // undefined behavior, probably! def. not what you want
```

…but in Rust [that’s a borrow-checker error][1]:

```rust
use std::fs::File;
use std::io::{BufRead, BufReader, Read};

fn main() -> std::io::Result<()> {
    let mut fr: File = File::open("/etc/hosts")?;
    let mut br: BufReader<File> = BufReader::new(fr);
    let mut line = String::new();
    br.read_line(&mut line)?;
    dbg!(line);
    fr.read(&mut [])?;
    Ok(())
}
```

```
error[E0382]: borrow of moved value: `fr`
  --> src/main.rs:10:5
   |
5  |     let mut fr: File = File::open("/etc/hosts")?;
   |         ------ move occurs because `fr` has type `std::fs::File`, which does not implement the `Copy` trait
6  |     let mut br: BufReader<File> = BufReader::new(fr);
   |                                                  -- value moved here
...
10 |     fr.read(&mut [])?;
   |     ^^ value borrowed here after move
```

For a `BufReader`, this is definitely a good thing, since it keeps an
internal buffer of extra data read from the file, and reading directly
from the underlying stream would break its invariants. What about for a
`TfRecordReader`? It feels like the same thing is true: if you just read
ahead in the file, then our bookkeeping about where in the record layout
we are will be incorrect. Preventing that is nice.

So, sure! Let’s let a `TfRecordReader` own an underlying reader.

There’s one place in our code where we _do_ want to poke the underlying
reader, though—in the documentation example for `read_record`. See: we
call `read_record(&mut Cursor::new(buf))`, then we change `buf`, then we
call it again with `&mut Cursor::new(buf)`, which is a different reader.
So one approach would be to expose

```rust
impl<R> TfRecordReader<R> {
    /// Borrows the underlying reader.
    pub fn as_inner(&mut self) -> &mut R {
        &mut self.reader
    }
}
```

which still lets our reader own the underlying reader but enables
callers to borrow it. Of course, if they borrow it, they can muck with
its state again, so we lose some of the guarantee that we’ve gained. So
instead what I’ve done is work around it a bit in the example, by
defining a reader type that actually does grow, just like a file does.
I’ve used [leading `#` comments][dt] to hide this part from the docs, so
that it still powers the doctest but doesn’t distract readers. You can
check out the doc server, if you like.

What do you think?

[1]: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2018&gist=bd57b5c90cd06bcc697cf355698b20cd
[dt]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/rustdoc/documentation-tests.html#hiding-portions-of-the-example

I am talking about the third place where we retry.

We have two code paths which retry via `_retry_obtain_certificate`, and those are triggered by errors from `newOrder` and `finalize`.

But we have a third code path which retries, triggered by one or more authorizations failing. But does not call `_retry_obtain_certificate`, so there is no logging:

https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/8c9b3027534ea65cd2f5f5845f153e35950d2a1c/certbot/certbot/_internal/client.py#L445-L458

There should be logging here, and it should be identical to the logging for the other two retry paths.

Alternatively, you could try to consolidate all of the retry logic into `_retry_obtain_certificate`, so that it will handle both cases:

- An ACME error with subproblems with identifiers, *or*
- One or more of the authorizations failed

```suggestion
**`aiormq`_**
-------------

`aiormq`_ is a pure python AMQP client library. It is under the hood of aio-pika and might to be used when you really loving works with the protocol low level.

Following examples demonstrates the user API.

Simple consumer
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

.. code-block:: python

    import asyncio
    import aiormq

    async def on_message(message):
        """
        on_message doesn't necessarily have to be defined as async.
        Here it is to show that it's possible.
        """
        print(f" [x] Received message {message!r}")
        print(f"Message body is: {message.body!r}")
        print("Before sleep!")
        await asyncio.sleep(5)   # Represents async I/O operations
        print("After sleep!")


    async def main():
        # Perform connection
        connection = await aiormq.connect("amqp://guest:guest@localhost/")

        # Creating a channel
        channel = await connection.channel()

        # Declaring queue
        declare_ok = await channel.queue_declare('helo')
        consume_ok = await channel.basic_consume(
            declare_ok.queue, on_message, no_ack=True
        )


    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
    loop.run_forever()


Simple publisher
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

.. code-block:: python
    :name: test_simple_publisher

    import asyncio
    from typing import Optional

    import aiormq
    from aiormq.abc import DeliveredMessage


    MESSAGE: Optional[DeliveredMessage] = None


    async def main():
        global MESSAGE

        body = b'Hello World!'

        # Perform connection
        connection = await aiormq.connect("amqp://guest:guest@localhost//")

        # Creating a channel
        channel = await connection.channel()

        declare_ok = await channel.queue_declare("hello", auto_delete=True)

        # Sending the message
        await channel.basic_publish(body, routing_key='hello')
        print(f" [x] Sent {body}")

        MESSAGE = await channel.basic_get(declare_ok.queue)
        print(f" [x] Received message from {declare_ok.queue!r}")


    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(main())

    assert MESSAGE is not None
    assert MESSAGE.routing_key == "hello"
    assert MESSAGE.body == b'Hello World!'

```

I agree. I don't think we need to define it as  packed.  We actually use packed for two different reasons: 
- First to ensure there is no padding bytes in between member variables.
- Second is to allow us to cast any pointer to our packed  type and not worry about or be restricted by the alignment requirements.

For the `Kek` type, we won't have either situation:
- Since the type is PoD (plain old data), C++ standard requires that a pointer to it to be same as pointer to to its first member variable. (so `&kek` and `kek.m8` should always be same).
- The `Tlv::ReadTlv()` method would not do any casting/mapping of types or pointers. It will copy the TLV content/value (from the message) as a byte sequence into the given buffer/pointer.

The CI tests are only against the cloud version, which seems to be the priority for this project. Given the limited resources available to maintain it, I would tend to agree with breaking older on-prem versions in favour of keeping track with the cloud and maintaining BC on a best-effort basis.

The GDPR compliance changes also represent breaking changes within a major version of the API. This represents a fair amount of work to restore functionality to the library as more things get deprecated and dropped. As far as I can tell it also has caused a fair amount of divergence between on-prem and cloud versions, increasing the difficulty of maintenance.

With these factors in mind I'd prefer to prioritize getting fixes in to bring CI back to green and raise issues that arise afterwards, so that the project can gain momentum again. With CI green, it should be easier to put forward smaller PRs that are easier to merge to fix BC piece-by-piece.

@carver Can I get your opinion on this?

I discovered a problem that we currently have in Py-EVM. Say you write header `10` as checkpoint header leading to a gap from `1` to `9`. At some point later I go and fetch the headers 1, 2, 3 from a peer with the intention to start closing the gap. Now the problem is, how do we know that the headers 1, 2, 3 do actually belong to the *canonical* chain? The call to `persist_header_chain` does validate the headers in the sense that they match the previous header (genesis, in this example) but it does not know if it will eventually lead us back to the header `10` that we accepted with a trusted score. It could also be an alternative history that we are accepting here. Eventually, as we are closing the gap at some point we will know if the headers match up to our expected header `10` but there is a window of uncertainty where we can't be exactly sure about it I believe.

I have two ideas how to go about that.

1. We could not assign block numbers until we finally closed the gap and then patch up the block numbers backwards from the closing point until we don't hit a missing one anymore. But that would mean that we can not read headers by block number while we are still working on closing the gap. We could still track the gap with the `header_db.get_header_chain_gaps()` API though.

2. We could add a safeguard that goes roughly like: If we are writing headers with a block number lower than the one of our current head and we do not have block numbers assigned to any headers for these block numbers yet, accept them as canonical headers even though we can not entirely be sure that these will eventually lead to our previously accepted trusted score header.

It was in  APPS-1390 where we tried to handle this case where we try to add this default value parsed from nextflow.config
```
ribo_database_manifest     = "${projectDir}/assets/rrna-db-defaults.txt"
```
Adding this explicitly would not work
```
nextflow run ...
'--ribo_database_manifest=${projectDir}/assets/rrna-db-defaults.txt' 
```
because the projectDir is not resolved when resolving this explicit param. Only when we added it to a runtime config it would work since projectDir is a configuration implicit variable
https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/script.html?highlight=projectdir#configuration-implicit-variables

However 
1. passing params via -c is not recommended in DSL 2 
2. this option is no longer needed in the nextflow run command, as we already make all schema inputs as OPTIONAL applet input. Thus when one schema option is not specified by user, its default value would be honored.

> Performs a SIMD compare of the packed unsigned byte, word integers in the second source operand and the first source operand and returns the maximum value for each pair of integers to the destination operand.
> Legacy SSE version PMAXUB: The source operand can be an MMX technology register or a 64-bit memory location. The destination operand can be an MMX technology register.
>
> 128-bit Legacy SSE version: The first source and destination operands are XMM registers. The second source
operand is an XMM register or a 128-bit memory location. Bits (MAX_VL-1:128) of the corresponding destination register remain unchanged.
> 
> VEX.128 encoded version: The first source and destination operands are XMM registers. The second source
operand is an XMM register or a 128-bit memory location. Bits (MAX_VL-1:128) of the corresponding destination register are zeroed.
> 
> VEX.256 encoded version: The second source operand can be an YMM register or a 256-bit memory location. The first source and destination operands are YMM registers.
>
> EVEX encoded versions: The first source operand is a ZMM/YMM/XMM register; The second source operand is a ZMM/YMM/XMM register or a 512/256/128-bit memory location. The destination operand is conditionally updated based on writemask k1.

> Also, the models accept float images, not uint8 images.

It is true the models receive float images that are also typically Normalized. These are not the versions of the images that you will use for visualization though. Most models scale to [0-1] while others recently require scaling to [-1,1] (see the SSD models). So at the end the user will need to keep around the non-normalized `uint8` version to visualize it.

An alternative course of action might be to continue handling all types of images but avoid any assumption over the color scales. You could do that by creating a palette only if the image is uint8 and expect the user to provide a valid list of colors on their right scales otherwise. SFrame left: key, A
SFrame right: Key, A, B
if we resolve in an order A->B, then B->C, the output of join on `Key` is Key, A, B, C.

but somehow we have the right frame built from a dict to be
SFrame right: Key, B, A.
Then it will raise exception:
> Key, A
> Key, A, B
> Key, A, B, <- adding A, conflict found, replace A to B, conflict found again, raise an exception. 

So the resolution depends on the order in SFrame. One way to resolve this is to make the operation order independent. The other is to make sfame construction deterministic.

Thanks for this PR. I'm concerned that users may also be using conda instead of or in addition to pip. What are the repercussions for a conda user if they read this and run pip install netCDF4, or put another way, should they take a different action?

- There is an older version of [netcdf4 on anaconda default channel](https://anaconda.org/anaconda/netcdf4), which still meets pvlib minimum requirments
- **WARNING** there is also a [conda-forge version of the current netcdf4](https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/netcdf4) however, the [netcdf4 conda-forge feedstock](../../../conda-forge/netcdf4-feedstock/blob/master/recipe/meta.yaml) lists numpy as a dependent, so mixing with the default anaconda channel may corrupt your conda env, therefore probably better to use an env with conda-forge as default
- the [netCDF4 `setup.py`](../../../Unidata/netcdf4-python/blob/master/setup.py) lists `numpy` and `cftime` as dependencies, so using `pip install netcdf4` will install `cftime` from PyPI not anaconda. I am assuming that the wheels at the Cheeseshop already include the hdf5 and netcdf4 libraries, which are also available from the anaconda main channel.

So my recommendation would be to treat LIKE ALIKE.
- if PyPI then use `pip`
- if anaconda, then use `conda`
- if conda-forge, then use `conda -c conda-forge`

Where this breaks down is for a few packages like siphon which AFAIK doesn't have a good version on the default anaconda channel. [Search anaconda for siphon](https://anaconda.org/search?q=siphon) and you find conda-forge, Unidata, and a few others, each with different versions. Siphon [`setup.py` lists `numpy`, `protobuf`, `beautifulsoup4`, `requests`, and `pandas` as dependencies](../../../Unidata/siphon/blob/master/setup.py#L17), which are all available from the default anaconda channel or conda-forge. If they are installed first using conda, then siphon can be installed from PyPI using pip.

Idea behind `float_meta` was to hint that it preserves the meta information whether the byte data is/was 32 or 64-bit. I think `float_native` or `float_numba` is better.

Your assumption is correct. Defaulting to `True` would produce more correct result in case of 32 bit floats as there's no fabricated decimal digits from native (64-bit) float representation. Problem with defaulting to `True` is as you already mentioned it exposes `numba.f4` and `numba.f8` types to the client and thus requires explicit support especially for `numba.f4`, see for example:

```
    def default(self, obj):
        if isinstance(obj, np.float32):
            d = dec.Decimal(np.format_float_positional(obj, precision=None, unique=True, trim='0'))
            ret = float(round(dec.Decimal(float(obj)), abs(d.as_tuple().exponent)))
            return ret
        # Let the base class default method raise the TypeError
        return json.JSONEncoder.default(self, obj)
```

`numba.f8` is at least some cases implicitly regarded as native Python float as they both are 64-bits. One possible approach to eliminate `numba` from the interface in all modes could be using the native `decimal` module, which is already utilized for imitating Haskell's rounding strategy.

`process_config(output[0])` is the same as doing this: 

```python
for config in output[:1]:
    process_config(output)
```

And if output is a list of only one item, it's the same as doing this, because it'll only loop once:

```python
for config in output:
    process_config(output)
```

Because remember, looping through a list like this is essentially the same as doing:

```python
for i in range(len(output)):
    process_config(output[i])
```

But if that's the flow you want for >1 as well, you can just use that for output, no matter if there are 0 items (it will loop zero times), 1 items (it will loop once), or 100 items. 

Anyway, it's all the same. You don't need the if/else.

I hear what you're saying. With this implementation, if the scheduler dies or becomes unresponsive we will never clean it up.

Today if the scheduler exits/dies it doesn't get recreated and the `DaskCluster` goes into a broken state. In #606 we discuss recreating the scheduler (probably using a `Deployment` or `ReplicaSet`). This is the opposite of what I understand you are proposing here which is cleaning up the whole `DaskCluster` if the scheduler is lost.

My view is that if a `DaskCluster` resource exists the controller should be working to get everything into a working state. We can achieve this via liveness/readiness probes and recreating missing `Pods`. If the scheduler or workers die it should be trying to reconcile and repair the cluster. We have some work to do to get to this place though.

I think actively reconciling the cluster and then asking the scheduler if it wants to time out is the right approach for this PR. But perhaps we also want some other timeout where if the controller can't get the cluster into a healthy state for a certain amount of time then it gives up and cleans it up.

However, I found a problem with `self._enabled` in Gunicorn applications. When Gunicorn spawns a child process, the flag `self._enabled` is still true, but the `TelemetryLogsMetricsWriter` is not running in the child process. In this PR, `TelemetryLogsMetricsWriter` starts correctly when applications spawn a new child process, and we validate `config._telemetry_metrics_enabled` each time we add a metric to avoid leaks.

Here, I limited the max MLR timeout to be about 24 days, which is not required by the Spec. 
This make expire time calculation simple, not having to cope with overflow, because the MLR Timeout in Milliseconds will not exceed the `kDistantFuture`. 

However it has two side-effects:

- Users can never set MLR timeout larger than `kMlrTimeoutMax` on a OpenThread BBR 
- OT devices that registers to BBR of another vendor would use `kMlrTimeoutMax` if the actual MLR timeout is larger than `kMlrTimeoutMax`. 

Personally I believe both side-effects are acceptable because a MLR timeout of 24 days should be more than enough for most situations. 

If MLR timeout is allowed to exceed the defined `kMlrTimeoutMax`, we can't not use `TimeMilli` for maintaining expire times because the expire time will be larger than `kDistantFuture`. We will have to use a per-second timestamp for expire time. However, currently we don't have an working implementation of per-second timer or timestamp.

In order to do execution, the `NlvrLanguage` object needs instance-specific state.  There is no way around that.  Once `NlvrLanguage` has instance-specific state, I don't know what else a `World` is in charge of that is separate from the `Language`.  The `World` used to have the logical form parsing / translation logic, but we don't need that anymore.  When I was rewriting things, I first kept the `World` object, but replaced everything in it with an underlying `Language` object.  What was left was a thin veneer that translated API calls from the names the old `World` used to what the new `Language` used, which was basically pointless.  I don't see what having a `World` class gives you.

Also consider the `WikiTablesLanguage`.  The instance-specific state affects the language itself.  The language isn't well defined in the absence of instance-specific state.  There's a continuum of languages here: the example `Arithmetic` language has no instance-specific anything, so the constructor doesn't need any parameters.  The `NlvrLanguage` has instance-specific execution, and because we use the executor to define the language itself, the `NlvrLanguage` object must have instance-specific state - without that, the whole re-write falls apart.  Then there's the `WikiTablesLanguage`, which has not only instance-specific execution, but also an instance-specific language.  Again, here, because of how this new API works, there is no `Language` without the instance-specific state.  So what should the `World` do?  There's nothing left for it to do, so I removed it.

```py
    def __setitem__(self, key, item):
        row_loc, col_loc, _, __, ___ = _parse_tuple(key)
        if isinstance(row_loc, list) and len(row_loc) == 1:
            if row_loc[0] not in self.qc.index:
                index = self.qc.index.insert(len(self.qc.index), row_loc[0])
                self.qc = self.qc.reindex(labels=index, axis=0)
                self.df._update_inplace(new_query_compiler=self.qc)

        if isinstance(col_loc, list) and len(col_loc) == 1 and col_loc[0] not in self.qc.columns:
            new_col = pandas.Series(index=self.df.index)
            new_col[row_loc] = item
            self.df.insert(loc=len(self.df.columns), column=col_loc[0], value=new_col)
            self.qc = self.df._query_compiler
        else:
            row_lookup, col_lookup = self._compute_lookup(row_loc, col_loc)
            super(_LocIndexer, self).__setitem__(row_lookup, col_lookup, item)

it's about the implementation of Path.samefile. It will try to resolve the 2 paths and then determine whether they point to the same file/directory. The good part is that, if `self` is a link to `other`, it will return `True`; the bad part is, if one of the base path is not an existent path, it will raise an Exception.

So here I made this change for 2 reasons:
1. actually we can always push base path here, so a soft condition to skip push bring no harm;
2. it will avoid resolving the base path in Schema. So even if the base path is a temp folder and is deleted before dumping, ValidationError will be raised and only raised when trying to dump a relative LocalPathField.

> So, my plan was to get rid of the `pypi_repository` as a build target, and instead have it be a subsystem, like I did for docker registries. wdyt?

This is probably fine. The primary challenge just becomes supporting multiple of them. We don't have a great razor for "what should go in targets" vs "what should go in config", but one potentially useful angle is: how many of them will there be?

If it's possible that many different projects in a repository will need to be published to many different places, then targets might be a better pattern. On the other hand, if the whole repository is likely to only publish to a handful of destinations (common for most language and docker packages probably... the most common number of destinations is probably 2: one for OSS, and one for closed source), then config is probably fine... although as soon as the number of destinations is >1, you need to have a structure in config that supports naming the destinations.

This was conceptually/structurally a big change that touched a lot of different bits and pieces, i.e., it was not an easy feat but ultimately/unfortunately resource constraints hindered its completion.

from gcloud.aio.datastore import Datastore

class MyKey:
  pass

class MyDatastore(Datastore):

The paginated experience feels more like a book because the content is usually rendered with two columns, with the exception of smaller viewports where it will transition to a single column. I feel that the paginated mode is superior for reading books and pure text documents since the readability is better, specifically because of the column layout.

The scrolled mode is definitely superior for documents with tables or inflexible flow typed content that doesn't morph to different layouts. We may find there are other situations where the scrolled mode is superior as well, but when it comes to books, I think it's a poorer experience. To render a book in scrolled mode, it would be preferable to limit the content width which wastes screen real-estate and essentially makes fullscreen mode mostly unnecessary.

I don't think it's confusing to have different modes. Notion essentially does this with tables inside pages, but contextualizes the scrolled vs. paginated/column mode by each block. Mixing the two in the same document I find actually less ideal, so this approach is the best of all options IMO.

No. The return type of `dotenv.load_dotenv()` is bool, never a`DotEnv`. More specifically it is a literal `True` value always.

If we want to keep behavior exactly as it was, then yes, we can `return None` and set the return type to `Optional[_BoolType]` in this MR, in which case the behavior doesn't change at all compared to what it currently is. This is a very confusing and inconsistent return value though: it means we return `None` if a recursive search was done, and no .env was found, and a literal `True` in all other cases (including the case when no .env was done and no recursive search was done). We never return a `False`. The return value is essentially quite meaningless.

Like said, if the return value is meaningless, it should IMO always be `None`. I can and would like to make that change, but it's a breaking change, so it's up to you.

This MR in its current state would change the return value to a literal `True` value, which is consistent with `dotenv.load_dotenv`.

I think we actually should also remove the argparse-facing default value for `--api_endpoint` too, because otherwise passing `--origin=https://staging.tensorboard.dev` will not behave as desired, since the `flags.api_endpoint` override behavior will still kick in and default to the prod API.  We can instead just set the hardcoded API endpoint as a fallback behavior used for the `create_server_info()` path only, which gets us closer to our desired final state.

Then we'd have the following code for now:

```python
_DEFAULT_ORIGIN = 'https://tensorboard.dev'
_HARDCODED_API_ENDPOINT = 'api.tensorboard.dev:443'

def _get_server_info(flags):
  origin = flags.origin or _DEFAULT_ORIGIN
  if not flags.origin:
    # Temporary fallback to hardcoded API endpoint in the unspecified case.
    api_endpoint = flags.api_endpoint or _HARDCODED_API_ENDPOINT
    return server_info_lib.create_server_info(origin, api_endpoint)
  # same from here down
```

And after the server-side rollout, we'd remove `_HARDCODED_API_ENDPOINT` and update it to:

```python
if not flags.origin and flags.api_endpoint:
  return server_info_lib.create_server_info(origin, flags.api_endpoint)
```

When using Docker with Ray, we need to run most commands inside the Docker container; e.g. `ray start`.

Currently this wrapping happens implicitly for (what was) `*_setup_commands` and `*_start_ray_commands`. This is problematic: some commands need to run outside of Docker (like mounting EFS instances on AWS), some commands need to run inside Docker (like updating source code of your application). Previously one could use `*_initialization_commands` to run things outside Docker. However, with the recently added support for caching stopped nodes this broke as `initialization_commands` will not get run on node restart.

See https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/6128 for discussion that led to this API.

I did some investigation into isbuiltin, getargspec, and getfullargspec. Here's some of what I learned:

A list of Python 2 builtin functions is available here: https://docs.python.org/2/library/functions.html
There are 76 items in the list.
inspect.isbuiltin only returns True for 48 of them, returns False for 27 of them, and cannot be called on 1 of them (print).

For Python 3 the list of builtin functions is at https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html
There are 68 items on this list. inspect.isbuilting gives True for 41, False for 26, and cannot be run on 1 (breakpoint).

For many of these builtins, getargspec and getfullargspec successfully return the argspec of the builtin functions. For these, we want to use those argspecs. So, it sounds to me like the behavior is actually better in 3.7 than in 3.6, and we should update the tests to allow the improved behavior rather than updating the code to enforce the worse behavior.

That said, I'm still not sure we shouldn't just use the `SR`. Since the user could have set the `sr` on the collection manually, the compromise solution here risks explicitly ignoring their intentions. This isn't an entirely benign decision, because in subsequent transformations, if a mismatch in sampling rates is detected, that will trigger a resampling in at least one variable, when potentially none might have been needed. They could also have code that depends on knowing what the SR is.

I think maybe we need to come up with a general policy about this, because it crops up in other places too (e.g., the issue about what to do if event files contain very short durations). I.e., the general question is, in a situation where the user appears to be picking sampling rates that are objectively inefficient or result in loss of information, should we be paternalistic and fix it for them, or just let them know they can probably do better?

There are four cases here:

(1) exc in self.retry_for, exc NOT in self_do_not_retry_for: this case is correct, and will retry

(2) exc in self.retry_for, exc in self.do_not_retry_for: this will retry the exception, and this is probably a user error, maybe check that users don't do this as a precondition in __init__?

(3) exc NOT in self.retry_for, exc in self.do_not_retry_for: this case is correct, and will not retry

(4) exc NOT in self.retry_for, exc NOT in self.do_not_retry_for: this case is sort of questionable. It will retry the exception, even though the user didn't explicitly ask for it to be retried. This is where we need a more clear contract for what users can expect from this logic.

> Is that the same strategy that metricbeat is using to convert prometheus histograms?

Good question. Looking at https://github.com/elastic/beats/blob/master/x-pack/metricbeat/module/prometheus/collector/histogram.go, no - there's some similarity, but it's not the same. Also I miswrote the rules above, now corrected.

Metricbeat is doing the following:
 - for +Inf "le", interpolate by adding half the difference between the previous two buckets to the second last bucket.
 - for the first bucket, use the midpoint from the bucket's value to zero. I think that's probably a bug, as it could lead to incorrect ordering of histogram values? e.g. if you have le=-100 then le=-99, then the first one will report as -50, and the second as -99.5
 - for all other buckets, use the midpoint the bucket and its predecessor

@exekias WDYT about changing Metricbeat's implementation to what I outlined in https://github.com/elastic/apm-agent-python/pull/1165#discussion_r651397014? Or otherwise, can you elaborate on the rationale behind the current implementation? We should probably all do the same thing. In retrospect, it would be helpful if Elasticsearch accepted bucket ranges :sweat_smile:

> Is that storage format optimizing for tdigest or hdrhistogram-based percentile aggregations?

t-digest. Prometheus histograms are general and may have negative values, which won't work with HDRHistogram.

- If someone changes `get_temp_directory()` function in ipykernel, it will break us anyway, `normpath()` or not. See the discussion in #86 

If we really want to add `normpath()`, we have to do it for both the filename and the startswith-pattern, otherwise it will break the currently functional logic on Windows.

Note that the original code also cannot seed on different device. Currently we didn't see any usage of such functionality, so we chose this solution for performance.

Intention behind this your change seems to make this method to return `None` if and only if a requested entity does not exist, right?  We should be able to distinguish server errors (Wikidata's fault) from non-existent entities.  However, catching every `HTTPError` and `URLError` could make Wikidata's temporary server errors to look like non-existent entities.

Therefore, this code should be adjusted to the following direction:

- Since `URLError` is too vague (it covers any kind of exceptions `urllib` can throw), I believe we should not catch this type of exceptions.
- There are two types of error codes in HTTP: 400 series (client errors) & 500 series (server errors).  And `HTTPError` represents both kinds of errors as I recall.  Therefore, we should not catch server errors here.  IMHO you'd better catch only exceptions with `e.code == 404` and re`raise` any others.

Here is my thoughts on Enh-ACK and `OT_RADIO_CAPS_TRANSMIT_SEC`. Like other radio capabilities we have in `SubMac`, a platform can choose to support it or not. If platform does not support one specific radio capability, `SubMac` should be able to provide an alternative.

We can change the API for `TxFrame::GenerateEnhAck` to pass one more parameter `aFrameCounter` in so that this function could be used as a utility function(like `TxFrame::ProcessTransmitAesCcm`) and is context free which means it could be called in interrupt context.

If Radio dose not have the capability `OT_RADIO_CAPS_TRANSMIT_SEC`, the received frame is passed up to `SubMac` in callback `SubMac::HandleReceiveDone`, this is already in OT context. `SubMac` can generate an Enh-ACK, encrypt it and send it out to radio. Increamenting the frame counter in same context avoid the racing condition. If radio supports `OT_RADIO_CAPS_TRANSMIT_SEC`, then it still can call the utility function `TxFrame::GenerateEnhAck` to generate an Enh-ACK, but the frame counter is maintained by radio layer so that radio will provide atomicity.

The function `otPlatRadioTxStartedInterrupt` is called when `SubMac` needs to update the CSL IE. We should not update frame counter in this.

I'd vote for either keeping the one-hot encoding as you had it before, with columns added directly into the DataFrame, until it becomes a problem:
```python
    def _transform_pandas(self, df: pd.DataFrame):
        _validate_df(df, *self.columns)
        # Compute new one-hot encoded columns
        for column in self.columns:
            column_values = self.stats_[f"unique_values({column})"]
            for column_value in column_values:
                df[f"{column}_{column_value}"] = (
                    df[column] == column_value
                ).astype(int)
        # Drop original unencoded columns.
        df = df.drop(columns=self.columns)
        return df
```
Or dropping columns progressively and in-place as the corresponding one-hot encoded columns are added:
```python
    def _transform_pandas(self, df: pd.DataFrame):
        _validate_df(df, *self.columns)
        # Compute new one-hot encoded columns
        for column in self.columns:
            column_values = self.stats_[f"unique_values({column})"]
            for column_value in column_values:
                df[f"{column}_{column_value}"] = (
                    df[column] == column_value
                ).astype(int)
            df.drop(columns=[column], inplace=True)
        return df
```

Agreed! I haven't thought of a good way to automatically cover this, since if we could automatically generate the complete array types that we need to cover, we'd directly use such a list instead of defining our own. We should also keep in mind that this will be a stopgap fix and that we fully intend to fix this in upstream Arrow before the 8.0.0 release, at which point we'll most likely require that as the minimum Arrow version.

In the meantime, there are the following cases for future breaks:
1. Our custom serialization breaks for one of these array types. We can boost our test coverage to cover all major array types (currently working on this) so this is caught in our CI when a new pyarrow version is released.
2. Arrow adds a new array type that's not in this list. Note that this won't cause a failure; instead, this will cause the default serialization path to be used. Since these array types will most likely be very niche, I think that addressing these as they come up should be fine.
3. Arrow removes an array type that's in this list. This should be caught in CI via a very loud `AttributeError` that will fail every Datasets test, so this will be easy to catch.

The biggest pain around (1) and (2) is that pushing out a new Datasets patch release carries a lot of overhead, so it would be great if we could ensure that such breaks don't block users. Here are some ideas for mitigating this issue:
a. Put a try-except around the custom serialization code; upon failure, log the error and fall back to the default serialization path.
b. Put the registering of the custom serializer behind an environment variable flag that defaults to being enabled. This way, if the user runs into one of these breaking cases that isn't fix by the try-except fallback, they can always completely disable the custom serializer.
c. Add a `_register_serializer_for_array_type()` hook, so if Arrow adds a new array type and it works with the common `pa.Array` base class path of our custom serializer (which it most likely will), a user can add a `_register_serializer_for_array_type(pa.lib.SomeNewArray)` before their Datasets code to try to leverage the custom serialization path.

I think that decent test coverage and doing (a), (b), and (c) should mitigate the pain caused by any of these breaks. What do you think?

Can't say if "always", but the `_datetime_to_json()` helper has been unchanged since 2017 and was supposedly doing its job fine with naive datetime objects in this time - at least I don't remember any reported issues related to timezones. The `strftime()` conversion just uses the `datetime` fields at their face value and ignores any `tzinfo` set.

Considering that naive datetimes are used most of the time (as opposed to timezone-aware datetime objects) and that users did not complain, the UTC timezone assumption was apparently correct behavior. The logic just failed to cover the case when somebody actually used a timezone-aware instance.

And indeed, unit tests expect the same, a naive `datetime` instance is supposed to be converted to a string representation with a zero offset from UTC ([example](https://github.com/googleapis/python-bigquery/blob/530e1e8d8fe8939e914a78ff1b220907c1b87af7/tests/unit/test__helpers.py#L729-L731)).

On this one, I could use some advice. In lineprogram header v4 and below, there are two arrays - directories, which is an array of strings, and files, which is an array of (name, directory_index, last_mod_time, size) tuples. These are stored in the lineprogram header object as two collections - `include_directory` and `file_entry`, respectively.

In v5, both arrays are present but they have a dynamic structure - they are each prefixed with an array of fields. So I could not reuse the old variable names, `include_directory` and `file_entry`, without either losing data or breaking item datatype. I've introduced two new data items in the header - `directories` and `file_names`. The names came straight from the DWARF5 spec. However, since the v5 data structures can be (but don't have to be) supersets of the v4 ones, I've provided code that would generate `include_directory` from `directories` and `file_entry` from `file_names` if the latter are present. This way, if an existing library consumer encounters a binary with lineprogram header v5, they will still see the familiar data structures.

This changes the externally visible API, but not in a breaking way. Let me know if I should have handled this differently. The fact that file names and directory names are data items (as opposed to, say, method calls) is a given.

`*test*` would not be good because it would eliminate any package that happened to contain the substring "test" anywhere in the name, regardless of word boundaries. This includes every subpackage of the root package. For instance if you had `dagster.core.bestest_module.ever` it would filter out `dagster.core.bestest_module` and `dagster.core.bestest_module.ever`. So it could punch random holes in the packaged code. Probably wouldn't hit anything now but not future-proof. Safest way to go is to exclude anything nested under the `<LIB_NAME>_tests` package.

BTW I think the counterintuitive (why we had it incorrect in the first place) package-finding behavior of `setuptools` is part of the reason why many projects now put their source in a root-level `src` folder or similar, more obvious how to configure it if you do that.

@AndrewFarley , i appreciate your thoroughness of response. Yes, i'm familiar with Kubernetes (less so with how we're using Luigi here to interact; although looks moderately straight forwards as a k8s job that we then just poll for its status). So when i say we should "ensure that a failing job behaves as expected", I'm really referring to ensuring that Luigi acknowledges a failing job and responds to it appropriately. What I don't want to see is that a Luigi job thinks it was successful when its respective Kubernetes job actually exited in an erroneous state (in state phase error, or possibly even evicted by the control plane and thus an unknown status).

In the case that a job is not found, it looks like `__get_job` will raise an AssertionError; whereas if the job state is "Failed" (from `__get_job_status`), then we'll still raise an AssertionError within `_track_job`).

So the place I think we're missing test coverage for (that we should) is to ensure we get an AssertionError when its job fails. That isn't testing K8s functions correctly; that's ensuring that our Luigi code identifies and responds to the k8s api responses correctly and doesn't falsely think we were successful or respond to error in a way we don't expect. 

The extent to which we do this can be a little checking that:
```

        self.assertRaises(RuntimeError, fail.run)

If the only app running on the node is the CLI (like in our case typically), then no accurate timing is needed for OT tasks for sure and 'blocking' is ok. > I will see if I can intelligently slice the input image and then use util.view_as_window inside the loop.

Note that `view_as_windows` creates a view, not a copy, using clever NumPy stride tricks. You *can* and should create the windows outside the loop — they are free. Only the reshape actually triggers a copy and costs memory!

If you are not familiar with NumPy stride tricks, you can check out these notebooks for details on how this works:

https://github.com/jni/scipy-japan-2019

It is a Damn Cool feature of NumPy. =D

> I didn't know about scipy.ndimage.generic_filter. I don't see the generalization (yet?) though.

Sure, GitHub Actions are usually pinned to a major version. (Some projects pin to minor, patch or even Git SHAs, but major is most common.)

New major versions indicate breaking changes, but with GitHub Actions, it's often nothing to worry about, and can be something like they're using a newer Node version internally.

So it's a good idea to update them once in a while, there can be new features, like [actions/setup-python added builtin caching](https://github.com/actions/setup-python#caching-packages-dependencies) at some point. And the older versions will stop working at some point, for example they may use APIs that are deprecated/removed, or using an unsupported version of Node.

The caller is responsible for any eventual type checks or actual execution.

It's important that it behave this way, or otherwise it wouldn't be able to be used both in the type checker (which doesn't eval the resulting structure, it only type checks it) and the interpreter (which would want to eval the result, and then do type enforcement on the value, not the type signature).

Here, `module_name` has two possible meanings:
1. a simple datamodule name (string used to register the class in `_data_modules`
2. a fully qualified class name

In the first case, a message should be logged that the datamodule name is not registered, plus a full list of registered names (to easily spot typos or registration issues).

Only in the second case (`module_name` contains `'.'`) the module should be tried to be loaded. That will avoid the following exception to be raised (which is pretty meaningless to the user) if `module_name` is not registered and does not contain a `.`:

    return _bootstrap._gcd_import(name[level:], package, level)

  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 950, in _sanity_check
ValueError: Empty module name
```

seen = set()
for parameter in [p for p in parameters if isinstance(p, dict)]:

    # Check missing name/location
    missing_attrs = [attr for attr in ("name", "in") if attr not in parameter]
    if missing_attrs:
        raise InvalidParameterError("Missing keys {} for parameter".format(missing_attrs))

    # OpenAPI spec doesn't allow for duplicated parameters
    # A unique parameter is defined by a combination of a name and location
    unique_key = (parameter["name"], parameter["in"])
    if unique_key in seen:
        raise DuplicateParameterError(
            "Duplicate parameter with name {} and location {}".format(parameter["name"], parameter["in"])
        )
    seen.add(unique_key)

    # Add "required" attribute to path parameters
    if parameter["in"] == path:
        parameter["required"] = True
```

In those batches, the e.g. OBS usually have structure `[BxT, h]` (h = obs dim), whereas the "state_in_0" key has a structure of only `[B, h]` (or even more complicated: `[BxT/[max-seq-len], h]`). If this new counting procedure now finds one of the state fields first and use the len of the data for its own "count", then we get the count wrong.
Rough fix would be to skip `state_(in|out)_\d+` keys, just like we do with INFOS.

Let's say we want to run the purge job and make sure the old documents are moved to a new location. Pseudocode to do that (haven't tested, just and idea how to do that):

```python
from thoth.storages import GraphDatabase
from thoth.storages import CephStore
from thoth.storages import SolverResultsStore
from thoth.common import datetime2datetime_str

graph = GraphDatabase()
graph.connect()

solver_store = SolverResultsStore()
solver_store.connect()

# Move to solver-purge-<timestamp>.
target_prefix = f"{solver_store.ceph.prefix.rsplit("/", maxsplit=1)[0]}/solver-purge-{datetime2datetime_str()}/"
target_store = CephStore(prefix=target_prefix)
target_store.connect()

for solver_document_id in solver_document_ids:
    document = solver_store.retrieve_document(document_id=solver_document_id)
    target_store.store_document(document, document_id=solver_document_id)
    solver_store.delete_document(document_id=solver_document_id)  # needs to be implemented in ceph.py
    graph.remove_solver_entry(document_id=solver_document_id)  # needs to be implemented in postgres.py
```

This logic can be refactored and placed to `GraphDatabase` in postgres.py which would accept `solver_store` and `target_prefix` to move documents based on the query and delete the entry in the database (returns number of documents purged so we can eventually track it):

```python
class GraphDatabase:

    def purge_solver_documents(
        solver_store: SolverResultsStore,
        target_prefix: str,
        *,
        os_name: Optional[str] = None,
        Optional[os_version] = None,
        python_version: Optional[str] = None
    ) -> int:
        ...
```
